introduction:
Entity–Relationship (ER) modeling is a conceptual database design technique used to model the structure 
      and relationships of data in a database system. The ER model provides a graphical representation of entities, 
      their attributes, and the relationships between entities. It serves as a bridge between real-world requirements 
      and the implementation of a relational database.

sections.title: Entities
sections.content:
An entity is a real-world object or concept that can be distinctly identified. Entities are represented 
        by rectangles in ER diagrams. Examples include Student, Course, Employee, Department, etc. Each entity 
        has a set of attributes that describe its properties.
sections.code:
Examples:
- Student (student_id, name, email)
- Course (course_id, title, credits)
- Employee (emp_id, name, salary)

sections.title: Attributes
sections.content:
Attributes are properties or characteristics of entities. They are represented by ovals or ellipses 
        connected to their entity. Attributes can be simple (atomic), composite (made of sub-attributes), 
        single-valued, multi-valued, or derived (calculated from other attributes).
sections.code:
Types:
- Simple: age, salary
- Composite: address (street, city)
- Multi-valued: phone_numbers
- Derived: age (from DOB)

sections.title: Relationships
sections.content:
A relationship represents an association between two or more entities. Relationships are represented 
        by diamonds in ER diagrams. They can be one-to-one (1:1), one-to-many (1:N), or many-to-many (M:N).
sections.code:
Examples:
- Student ENROLLS Course (M:N)
- Department HAS Employee (1:N)
- Person OWNS Passport (1:1)

sections.title: Cardinality
sections.content:
Cardinality specifies the number of instances of one entity that can be associated with instances 
        of another entity. It is represented by numbers (1, N, M) on relationship lines. Common cardinalities 
        include 1:1, 1:N, and M:N.

sections.title: Participation Constraints
sections.content:
Participation constraints specify whether the existence of an entity depends on its relationship 
        with another entity. Total participation (double line) means every entity must participate, while 
        partial participation (single line) means some entities may not participate.
sections.code:
Examples:
- Every employee MUST belong to a department (total)
- Not every student enrolls in a course (partial)

sections.title: Weak Entities
sections.content:
A weak entity is an entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its attributes alone. It depends 
        on a strong (owner) entity and is represented by a double rectangle. The relationship between 
        weak and strong entities is called an identifying relationship.
sections.code:
Example:
- Dependant (weak) depends on 
  Employee (strong entity)
- Primary key includes owner's key

practicalExample.title: Practical Example
practicalExample.description: Consider a university database with entities: Student, Course, and Department
practicalExample.code:
ER Diagram:
- Student (student_id, name, email)
- Course (course_id, title, credits)
- Department (dept_id, dept_name)
- Student ENROLLS Course (M:N)
- Department OFFERS Course (1:N)

Relational Schema:
Student(student_id, name, email, dept_id)
Course(course_id, title, credits, dept_id)
Department(dept_id, dept_name)
Enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade)

realWorldApplication.title: Designing a Hospital System
realWorldApplication.scenario: Before writing a single line of code for a Hospital, engineers sit with doctors to draw an ER Diagram. They map out 'Entities' (Patients, Doctors, Wards) and 'Relationships' (A Doctor *treats* 1 to Many Patients). If they get this blueprint wrong, a patient might get assigned to a room that doesn't exist!
